title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: networks created equal. Many real-world networks bipartite, meaning two distinct types nodes, ties form types, never within . Students enroll courses. Countries sign treaties. Legislators join committees. Users purchase products. case, adjacency matrix rectangular rather square, row column nodes play fundamentally different roles. lame package supports bipartite networks ame() (cross-sectional) lame() (longitudinal). key difference unipartite case model uses separate latent spaces two node types, connected interaction matrix GG captures row-node column-node characteristics relate .

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: bipartite network, probability tie row node ii column node jj modeled : yij=β′xij+ai+bj+ui′Gvj+ϵijy_{ij} = \beta'x_{ij} + a_i + b_j + u_i'Gv_j + \epsilon_{ij} additive effects work just unipartite case: aia_i captures “active” row node ii (e.g., student takes many courses), bjb_j captures “popular” column node jj (e.g., course high enrollment). multiplicative term ui′Gvju_i'Gv_j different. Instead ui′vju_i'v_j shared latent dimensions, row column nodes live potentially different-dimensional latent spaces (RrowR_\text{row} RcolR_\text{col}), interaction matrix GG maps . flexible, letting model discover , say, two dimensions student preferences map onto three dimensions course characteristics. things drop away bipartite setting. dyadic correlation parameter ρ\rho, ties inherently directional row column nodes (can’t reciprocal tie student–course network). additive effects aa bb also independent variances rather joint covariance, since describe fundamentally different types actors.

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: illustrate workflow, simulate bipartite network 30 students 20 courses. student 2-dimensional latent position captures academic preferences, course 2-dimensional latent position captures kind student attracts. interaction matrix GG determines latent dimensions combine predict enrollment.
code: # Simulate a student-course enrollment network n_students <- 30 n_courses <- 20  # True latent positions (unobserved in practice) U_true <- matrix(rnorm(n_students * 2), n_students, 2) V_true <- matrix(rnorm(n_courses * 2), n_courses, 2)  # Interaction matrix: dimension 1 has positive affinity, # dimension 2 has negative (students high on dim 2 avoid courses high on dim 2) G_true <- matrix(c(1, 0.5, 0.5, -1), 2, 2)  # Generate enrollment probabilities and binary outcomes eta <- U_true %*% G_true %*% t(V_true) prob <- plogis(eta) Y_bipartite <- matrix(rbinom(n_students * n_courses, 1, prob),                       n_students, n_courses)  rownames(Y_bipartite) <- paste0("Student", 1:n_students) colnames(Y_bipartite) <- paste0("Course", 1:n_courses)  cat("Network dimensions:", dim(Y_bipartite), "\n") #> Network dimensions: 30 20 cat("Enrollment rate:", round(mean(Y_bipartite), 2), "\n") #> Enrollment rate: 0.54

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: Fitting bipartite model requires setting mode = "bipartite" specifying latent dimensions node type separately via R_row R_col. output includes posterior means student latent positions (U), course latent positions (V), interaction matrix (G), along additive effects: APM student activity levels BPM course popularity.
code: # Note: burn/nscan are kept small here for fast vignette building. # For real analyses, use burn >= 1000 and nscan >= 5000. fit_cross <- ame(   Y = Y_bipartite,   mode = "bipartite",   R_row = 2,              # latent dimensions for students   R_col = 2,              # latent dimensions for courses   family = "binary",   burn = 100,   nscan = 500,   odens = 5,   print = FALSE )  summary(fit_cross) #>  #> === AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ a[i] + b[j], family = 'binary'" #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>       Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper   #> beta0    0.136    0.167   0.815   0.415   -0.192    0.464   #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.248    0.093 #> cab    0.000    0.000 #> vb     0.334    0.112 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #> rho    0.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) #>   Note: bipartite model (rho fixed to 0, cab fixed to 0)

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: uv_plot function layout = "biplot" places row column nodes latent space, making easy see students cluster near courses. Students courses appear close together plot higher predicted probability tie.  interaction matrix GG tells us latent dimensions relate . Positive entries mean students courses score similarly dimension likely connected; negative entries mean opposite.
code: uv_plot(fit_cross, layout = "biplot") +   ggtitle("Bipartite Latent Space: Students and Courses") G_melt <- melt(fit_cross$G) ggplot(G_melt, aes(x = Var2, y = Var1, fill = value)) +   geom_tile() +   scale_fill_gradient2(low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red") +   labs(title = "Estimated Interaction Matrix G",        x = "Column Dimension", y = "Row Dimension") +   theme_minimal()

text: bipartite networks observed multiple time periods (say, student enrollments across semesters, country–treaty memberships across decades), lame() lets pool information across time optionally allowing latent structure evolve.

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: simulate panel user–item interaction networks 5 periods. underlying latent structure held fixed (now), small random shocks overall baseline period.
code:
n_periods <- 5 n_users <- 20 n_items <- 15  U_long <- matrix(rnorm(n_users * 2), n_users, 2) V_long <- matrix(rnorm(n_items * 2), n_items, 2) G_long <- matrix(c(1, 0.3, 0.3, -0.8), 2, 2)  Y_list <- list() for(t in 1:n_periods) {   eta_t <- U_long %*% G_long %*% t(V_long) + rnorm(1, 0, 0.2)   prob_t <- plogis(eta_t)   Y_list[[t]] <- matrix(rbinom(n_users * n_items, 1, prob_t),                         n_users, n_items)   rownames(Y_list[[t]]) <- paste0("User", 1:n_users)   colnames(Y_list[[t]]) <- paste0("Item", 1:n_items) } names(Y_list) <- paste0("T", 1:n_periods)  cat("Time periods:", length(Y_list), "
") #> Time periods: 5 cat("Dimensions per period:", dim(Y_list[[1]]), "
") #> Dimensions per period: 20 15 cat("Average density:", round(mean(sapply(Y_list, mean)), 2), "
") #> Average density: 0.51

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: simplest longitudinal model assumes latent positions additive effects constant time. pools time periods together get single estimate actor’s latent position, useful believe underlying structure stable just want data estimate precisely.
code: # Note: iterations are kept small for vignette speed; use burn >= 1000 # and nscan >= 5000 for real analyses. fit_static <- lame(   Y = Y_list,   mode = "bipartite",   R_row = 2,   R_col = 2,   family = "binary",   dynamic_uv = FALSE,   dynamic_ab = FALSE,   burn = 100,   nscan = 500,   odens = 5,   print = FALSE,   plot = FALSE )  summary(fit_static) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> NULL #>  #> Time periods: 5  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: bipartite  #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper   #> intercept    0.059    0.172   0.344   0.731   -0.278    0.396   #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.231    0.084 #> cab    0.000    0.000 #> vb     0.270    0.094 #> rho    0.000    0.000 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance)

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: suspect latent structure changes time (users’ tastes shift, items gain lose popularity), dynamic model lets latent positions additive effects drift via AR(1) processes. high autoregressive parameter (ρ\rho close 1) means slow, gradual change; low one means structure volatile period period.
code: # Same reduced iterations as above for vignette speed. fit_dynamic <- lame(   Y = Y_list,   mode = "bipartite",   R_row = 2,   R_col = 2,   family = "binary",   dynamic_uv = TRUE,   dynamic_ab = TRUE,   burn = 100,   nscan = 500,   odens = 5,   print = FALSE,   plot = FALSE )  summary(fit_dynamic) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> NULL #>  #> Time periods: 5  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: bipartite  #> Dynamic latent positions: enabled (rho_uv = 0.391 ) #> Dynamic additive effects: enabled (rho_ab = 0.258 ) #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper   #> intercept   -0.005    0.037  -0.146   0.884   -0.077    0.066   #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.223    0.070 #> cab    0.000    0.000 #> vb     0.278    0.114 #> rho    0.000    0.000 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance)

text: dynamic effects, uv_plot can show trajectories, illustrating actor’s latent position moves space time. line traces one actor’s path first last period, revealing actors’ roles stable shifting.  Convergence diagnostics especially important dynamic models, since additional parameters (ρ\rho, innovation variances) need adequate samples well-estimated.
code: uv_plot(fit_dynamic, plot_type = "trajectory") +   ggtitle("Dynamic Latent Positions: Trajectories Over Time") trace_plot(fit_dynamic)

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: natural question whether dynamic model worth added complexity. goodness--fit plots provide one way compare: dynamic model’s posterior predictive distributions better cover observed statistics, extra flexibility paying .   can also compare GOF statistics directly. bipartite networks, key statistics standard deviation row means (much users vary activity), standard deviation column means (much items vary popularity), four-cycle count (measure clustering bipartite networks, analogous transitivity unipartite networks).
code: # GOF plots for each model gof_plot(fit_static) gof_plot(fit_dynamic) gof_static <- fit_static$GOF gof_dynamic <- fit_dynamic$GOF  gof_df <- data.frame(   value = c(     colMeans(gof_static$sd.rowmean), colMeans(gof_dynamic$sd.rowmean),     colMeans(gof_static$sd.colmean), colMeans(gof_dynamic$sd.colmean),     colMeans(gof_static$four.cycles), colMeans(gof_dynamic$four.cycles)   ),   model = rep(c("Static", "Dynamic"), 3,               each = ncol(gof_static$sd.rowmean)),   statistic = rep(c("Row Mean SD", "Column Mean SD", "Four-Cycles"),                   each = 2 * ncol(gof_static$sd.rowmean)) )  ggplot(gof_df, aes(x = model, y = value, fill = model)) +   geom_boxplot() +   scale_fill_manual(values = c(Dynamic = "#D94A4A", Static = "#4A90D9")) +   facet_wrap(~statistic, scales = "free_y") +   labs(title = "GOF Comparison: Static vs Dynamic",        x = "Model", y = "GOF Statistic") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "none")

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: number latent dimensions (R_row, R_col) controls rich multiplicative structure . dimensions model can’t capture latent clustering; many risk overfitting (slower computation). practical approach fit models different dimensions compare GOF statistics. Smaller GOF values (closer zero) indicate better fit. Look point adding dimensions stops improving GOF appreciably.
code: dims_to_test <- list(   c(1, 1),   c(2, 2) )  gof_results <- list() for(i in seq_along(dims_to_test)) {   fit_temp <- ame(     Y = Y_bipartite,     mode = "bipartite",     R_row = dims_to_test[[i]][1],     R_col = dims_to_test[[i]][2],     family = "binary",     burn = 100,     nscan = 500,     odens = 5,     print = FALSE   )   gof_results[[i]] <- c(     R_row = dims_to_test[[i]][1],     R_col = dims_to_test[[i]][2],     gof_rowmean = mean(abs(fit_temp$GOF[, "sd.rowmean"])),     gof_fourcycles = mean(abs(fit_temp$GOF[, "four.cycles"]))   ) }  do.call(rbind, gof_results) #>      R_row R_col gof_rowmean gof_fourcycles #> [1,]     1     1   0.1422260       7239.300 #> [2,]     2     2   0.1409541       7266.175

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: use bipartite models. Use bipartite mode whenever network two fundamentally different types nodes ties form types. adjacency matrix rectangular, almost certainly need . Even happens square (e.g., 20 students 20 courses), set mode = "bipartite" row column nodes represent different kinds entities. Interpreting output. additive effects (APM, BPM) direct interpretation: tell row nodes unusually active column nodes unusually popular, controlling covariates latent structure. latent positions (U, V) interaction matrix (G) together capture residual patterns association, answering question “beyond covariates overall activity levels, row nodes tend connect column nodes?”

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: Bayesian model, always check convergence interpreting results. trace_plot function shows MCMC traces posterior densities regression coefficients variance components.  Look traces mix well (long flat stretches slow drifts) densities smooth unimodal. short chains used vignette, convergence guaranteed. practice, run longer chains consider multiple starting values.
code: trace_plot(fit_cross)

title: Static AME Models: Theory and Application
text: Additive Multiplicative Effects (AME) model provides framework analyzing network data accounting complex dependencies inherent relational observations. vignette demonstrates use static AME models lame package.

title: Static AME Models: Theory and Application
text: Standard regression models assume independent observations, network data violates assumption several ways: Row dependencies: Individuals vary propensity form outgoing ties Column dependencies: Individuals vary propensity receive ties Dyadic dependencies: presence tie (,j)(,j) may correlate tie (j,)(j,) Third-order dependencies: Transitivity clustering patterns

title: Static AME Models: Theory and Application
text: AME model network 𝐘\mathbf{Y} entries yijy_{ij} takes form: Yij∼F(ηij)Y_{ij} \sim F(\eta_{ij}) FF belongs exponential family linear predictor : ηij=μ+𝛃T𝐱ij+ai+bj+𝐮iT𝐯j+ϵij\eta_{ij} = \mu + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{x}_{ij} + a_i + b_j + \mathbf{u}_i^T \mathbf{v}_j + \epsilon_{ij}

title: Static AME Models: Theory and Application
text: Fixed Effects: - μ∈ℝ\mu \\mathbb{R}: intercept term - 𝛃∈ℝp\boldsymbol{\beta} \\mathbb{R}^p: regression coefficients pp covariates - 𝐱ij∈ℝp\mathbf{x}_{ij} \\mathbb{R}^p: covariate vector dyad (,j)(,j) Random Effects: - ai∼𝒩(0,σa2)a_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_a^2): sender/row effects - bj∼𝒩(0,σb2)b_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_b^2): receiver/column effects Multiplicative Effects: - 𝐮i,𝐯j∈ℝR\mathbf{u}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \\mathbb{R}^R: latent factors RR-dimensional space - 𝐮iT𝐯j=∑r=1Ruirvjr\mathbf{u}_i^T \mathbf{v}_j = \sum_{r=1}^{R} u_{ir} v_{jr}: inner product capturing latent homophily Dyadic Correlation: (ϵijϵji)∼𝒩(𝟎,(σϵ2ρσϵ2ρσϵ2σϵ2))\begin{pmatrix} \epsilon_{ij} \\ \epsilon_{ji} \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{0}, \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_\epsilon^2 & \rho\sigma_\epsilon^2 \\ \rho\sigma_\epsilon^2 & \sigma_\epsilon^2 \end{pmatrix}\right) ρ\rho captures reciprocity.

title: Static AME Models: Theory and Application
text: package uses Bayesian inference via MCMC sample posterior distribution: p(𝛉|𝐘)∝p(𝐘|𝛉)⋅p(𝛉)p(\boldsymbol{\theta} | \mathbf{Y}) \propto p(\mathbf{Y} | \boldsymbol{\theta}) \cdot p(\boldsymbol{\theta}) 𝛉\boldsymbol{\theta} contains model parameters.

title: Static AME Models: Theory and Application
text: section analyzes friendship networks AddHealth study using AME framework. Network Statistics
code: library(lame) library(ggplot2) library(gridExtra) library(reshape2) set.seed(123)  # Load data data(addhealthc3) # Convert valued network to binary (Y > 0 indicates a friendship) Y <- (addhealthc3$Y > 0) * 1 X_nodes <- addhealthc3$X    # Network dimensions n <- nrow(Y) n_edges <- sum(Y, na.rm = TRUE) density <- mean(Y, na.rm = TRUE)  # Basic statistics out_degree <- rowSums(Y, na.rm = TRUE) in_degree <- colSums(Y, na.rm = TRUE) reciprocal_pairs <- sum(Y * t(Y), na.rm = TRUE) / 2 reciprocity <- reciprocal_pairs / sum(Y, na.rm = TRUE)  # Create a summary table network_stats <- data.frame(   Statistic = c("Nodes", "Edges", "Density", "Mean degree", "Degree SD", "Reciprocity"),   Value = c(n, n_edges, round(density, 3), round(mean(out_degree), 2),              round(sd(out_degree), 2), round(reciprocity, 3)) ) knitr::kable(network_stats, caption = "Network Statistics")

title: Static AME Models: Theory and Application
text: Dyadic covariates capture homophily heterophily patterns: Dyadic Covariate Rates
code: # Homophily indicators: I(x_i = x_j) same_female <- outer(X_nodes[,"female"], X_nodes[,"female"], "==") * 1 same_race <- outer(X_nodes[,"race"], X_nodes[,"race"], "==") * 1 same_grade <- outer(X_nodes[,"grade"], X_nodes[,"grade"], "==") * 1  # Absolute difference: |x_i - x_j| grade_diff <- abs(outer(X_nodes[,"grade"], X_nodes[,"grade"], "-"))  # Combine covariates Xdyad <- array(NA, dim = c(n, n, 4)) Xdyad[,,1] <- same_female Xdyad[,,2] <- same_race   Xdyad[,,3] <- same_grade Xdyad[,,4] <- grade_diff dimnames(Xdyad)[[3]] = c('same_female', 'same_race', 'same_grade', 'grade_diff')  # Remove diagonal for(k in 1:4) diag(Xdyad[,,k]) <- NA  # Nodal covariates Xrow <- X_nodes[, c("female", "grade")] Xcol <- X_nodes[, c("female", "grade")]  # Covariate summary cov_stats <- data.frame(   Covariate = c("Same gender", "Same race", "Same grade", "Grade difference"),   Mean = round(c(mean(same_female, na.rm=TRUE),                   mean(same_race, na.rm=TRUE),                  mean(same_grade, na.rm=TRUE),                  mean(grade_diff, na.rm=TRUE)), 3) ) knitr::kable(cov_stats, caption = "Dyadic Covariate Rates")

title: Static AME Models: Theory and Application
text: full model includes fixed effects covariates, random effects individual heterogeneity, multiplicative effects latent structure: Φ−1(P(Yij=1))=μ+∑k=14βkdyadxij(k)+∑k=12βkrowxi(k)+∑k=12βkcolxj(k)+ai+bj+𝐮iT𝐯j\Phi^{-1}(P(Y_{ij} = 1)) = \mu + \sum_{k=1}^{4} \beta_k^{\text{dyad}} x_{ij}^{(k)} + \sum_{k=1}^{2} \beta_k^{\text{row}} x_i^{(k)} + \sum_{k=1}^{2} \beta_k^{\text{col}} x_j^{(k)} + a_i + b_j + \mathbf{u}_i^T \mathbf{v}_j

title: Static AME Models: Theory and Application
text: AME model uses conjugate weakly informative priors default. Custom priors starting values can specified:
code: # Example: Setting custom priors and starting values  # Prior means for regression coefficients prior_mean_beta <- rep(0, 9)  # 9 coefficients in our model  # Prior variance (inverse precision) for coefficients   prior_var_beta <- rep(10, 9)  # Diffuse prior with variance 10  # Prior for variance components prior_nu <- 3     # Degrees of freedom for inverse-Wishart prior_s2 <- 1     # Scale for variance components  # Starting values can be specified to improve convergence # Example: Use OLS-type estimates as starting values start_intercept <- qnorm(mean(Y, na.rm = TRUE))  # Probit transform of density  # Create prior list prior_list <- list(   beta_mean = prior_mean_beta,   beta_var = prior_var_beta,   nu = prior_nu,   s2 = prior_s2 )  # Create starting values list start_vals <- list(   beta = c(start_intercept, rep(0, 8)),  # Start other coefficients at 0   s2a = var(rowMeans(Y, na.rm = TRUE)),  # Row variance from data   s2b = var(colMeans(Y, na.rm = TRUE)),  # Column variance from data   rho = 0.1  # Small positive reciprocity ) # Fit AME model with sufficient iterations for convergence # Note: prior and start_vals can be passed as arguments fit <- ame(Y,            Xdyad = Xdyad,           Xrow = Xrow,           Xcol = Xcol,           R = 2,            # 2D latent space           family = "binary",           rvar = TRUE,      # row variance           cvar = TRUE,      # column variance           dcor = TRUE,      # dyadic correlation           burn = 500,       # burn-in (increase for publication-quality results)           nscan = 2000,     # sampling iterations           odens = 25,       # thinning interval           print = TRUE,           gof = TRUE           # prior = prior_list,     # Custom priors (commented out for default)           # start_vals = start_vals # Custom starting values (commented out)           )  # Model summary fit #>  #> Additive and Multiplicative Effects (AME) Model #> ================================================ #>  #> Network dimensions:  32 x 32  #> MCMC iterations:  80  #> Family:  binary  #> Mode:  unipartite  #> Symmetric:  FALSE  #>  #> Number of parameters: #>   Regression coefficients:  9  #>   Row/sender effects: enabled #>   Column/receiver effects: enabled #>   Dyadic correlation: enabled #>   Multiplicative effects dimension:  2  #>  #> Use summary(object) for detailed results

title: Static AME Models: Theory and Application
text: regression coefficients (𝛃\boldsymbol{\beta}): - Default: βk∼𝒩(0,10)\beta_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 10) (weakly informative) - Informative: Use smaller variance (e.g., 1) prior knowledge exists - binary networks, coefficients typically range -3 3 variance components (σa2,σb2\sigma^2_a, \sigma^2_b): - Default: Inverse-Gamma small shape scale - Controls heterogeneity individual effects - Larger prior variance allows individual variation dyadic correlation (ρ\rho): - Default: Uniform(-1, 1) Beta-transformed - Positive values expected social networks - Can constrain [0, 1] reciprocity expected

title: Static AME Models: Theory and Application
text: Good starting values accelerate convergence: Intercept: Use probit/logit transform network density Covariate effects: Start 0 use GLM estimates Variance components: Use empirical variance row/column means Latent positions: Random normal SVD residual matrix Reciprocity: Start observed reciprocity rate

title: Static AME Models: Theory and Application
text: goodness--fit statistics compare observed network features posterior predictive distribution:
code:

")      # Handle different row naming conventions   row_names <- rownames(var_comp)      # Sender variance   sender_idx <- grep("va", row_names, ignore.case = TRUE)[1]   if(!is.na(sender_idx)) {     cat("  Sender variance σ²ₐ =", round(var_comp[sender_idx, 1], 3),          "- variation in outgoing ties

text: Confusion Matrix Classification Performance
code: # Generate predictions pred_link <- predict(fit, type = "link")      # Linear predictor η pred_resp <- predict(fit, type = "response")  # Probability Φ(η)  # Classification performance Y_vec <- as.vector(Y) pred_vec <- as.vector(pred_resp) keep <- !is.na(Y_vec)  threshold <- 0.5 pred_binary <- (pred_vec > threshold) * 1 confusion <- table(Actual = Y_vec[keep], Predicted = pred_binary[keep])  # Confusion Matrix knitr::kable(confusion, caption = "Confusion Matrix") # Metrics accuracy <- sum(diag(confusion)) / sum(confusion) sensitivity <- confusion[2,2] / sum(confusion[2,]) specificity <- confusion[1,1] / sum(confusion[1,]) precision <- confusion[2,2] / sum(confusion[,2])  # Performance metrics performance <- data.frame(   Metric = c("Accuracy", "Sensitivity", "Specificity", "Precision", "AUC"),   Value = round(c(accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, NA), 3) )  # AUC calculation calc_auc <- function(pred, actual) {   pos_scores <- pred[actual == 1]   neg_scores <- pred[actual == 0]      # Count pairs where positive > negative   correct_pairs <- 0   total_pairs <- 0      for(pos in pos_scores) {     for(neg in neg_scores) {       if(pos > neg) correct_pairs <- correct_pairs + 1       if(pos == neg) correct_pairs <- correct_pairs + 0.5       total_pairs <- total_pairs + 1     }   }      return(correct_pairs / total_pairs) }  auc <- calc_auc(pred_vec[keep], Y_vec[keep]) performance$Value[5] <- round(auc, 3) knitr::kable(performance, caption = "Classification Performance")

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: Networks change. Countries close allies decade ago may drifted apart. legislator’s co-sponsorship patterns shift gain seniority switch committee assignments. user’s purchasing habits evolve tastes change. standard AME model assumes actor fixed latent position fixed additive effects across time periods. reasonable starting point (pooling across time gives data precise estimates), can miss important dynamics. lame package provides two mechanisms letting model capture temporal change: dynamic_uv time-varying latent positions dynamic_ab time-varying additive effects. vignette explains options , use , interpret results.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: standard AME model longitudinal data, tie actors ii jj time tt : yij,t=β′xij,t+ai+bj+ui′vj+ϵij,ty_{ij,t} = \beta'x_{ij,t} + a_i + b_j + u_i'v_j + \epsilon_{ij,t} covariates (xij,tx_{ij,t}) can vary time, everything else (sender effect aia_i, receiver effect bjb_j, latent positions uiu_i vjv_j) constant. means model assumes country’s tendency sanction others, position latent “sanctioning space,” 1993 2000.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: set dynamic_uv = TRUE, actor’s latent position evolves time according AR(1) process: Ui,k,t=ρuvUi,k,t−1+ϵi,k,tU_{,k,t} = \rho_{uv} \, U_{,k,t-1} + \epsilon_{,k,t}Vj,k,t=ρuvVj,k,t−1+ηj,k,tV_{j,k,t} = \rho_{uv} \, V_{j,k,t-1} + \eta_{j,k,t} autoregressive parameter ρuv\rho_{uv} controls persistent positions . value close 1 means positions change slowly, last year’s position strong predictor year’s. value close 0 means positions essentially re-drawn period. practice, ρuv\rho_{uv} estimated data, don’t need choose . useful believe underlying community structure evolving: alliances shift, social groups re-form, trading blocs realign.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: set dynamic_ab = TRUE, sender receiver effects evolve time: ai,t=ρabai,t−1+ϵi,ta_{,t} = \rho_{ab} \, a_{,t-1} + \epsilon_{,t}bj,t=ρabbj,t−1+ηj,tb_{j,t} = \rho_{ab} \, b_{j,t-1} + \eta_{j,t} captures changes actors’ overall activity levels. country might become active sanctioner change government. student might become less socially active across semesters. changes much actor participates, connect (’s dynamic_uv captures). can use either option alone combine . practice, dynamic_ab often good place start, since changes overall activity common relatively easy estimate. dynamic_uv adds flexibility also parameters.

text: dynamic effects implemented C++ via Rcpp RcppArmadillo using forward-filtering backward-sampling (FFBS) algorithm. standard approach state-space models: forward pass computes predictive distributions p(xt|x1:t−1)p(x_t | x_{1:t-1}), backward pass samples smoothed distribution p(x1:T|y1:T)p(x_{1:T} | y_{1:T}).

title: lame Overview
text: lame package provides tools fitting Longitudinal Additive Multiplicative Effects models network data observed time. study relationships actors (countries trading , legislators co-sponsoring bills, students forming friendships across semesters) repeated observations relationships, lame designed . modeling approach builds Additive Multiplicative Effects (AME) framework developed Peter Hoff, whose amen package (GitHub) provides foundational implementation cross-sectional network analysis. lame package extends framework several directions: Longitudinal replicated data: amen focuses single networks (ame()) basic replicated designs (ame_rep()), lame built ground panel data, networks observed across multiple time periods, potentially actors entering leaving sample. Dynamic effects: Actors’ positions latent space baseline activity levels can evolve time via AR(1) processes (dynamic_uv dynamic_ab), letting model network structure changes rather assuming fixed. C++ acceleration: Core sampling routines implemented Rcpp/RcppArmadillo faster computation, matters many time periods actors. Bipartite networks: Explicit support two-mode networks (e.g., countries treaties, users products) separate latent spaces row column nodes. ggplot2-based diagnostics: Built-visualization functions (trace_plot, gof_plot, ab_plot, uv_plot) produce publication-ready ggplot2 figures. Standard S3 methods: coef(), confint(), fitted(), residuals(), predict(), simulate() work expect R model object. package part netify-verse, works seamlessly netify package data preparation.

title: lame Overview
text: see lame works practice, analyze data Threat Imposition Sanctions (TIES) dataset (Morgan et al. 2014). data cover 35 countries across four years (1993–1995 2000). core question straightforward: country-level dyad-level factors predict whether one country sanctions another, latent patterns sanctioning behavior beyond covariates explain? dataset includes: Y: list directed binary adjacency matrices (one per year). Entry yij,t=1y_{ij,t} = 1 means country ii imposed sanctions country jj year tt. Xdyad: Dyadic covariates (geographic distance number shared intergovernmental organizations (IGOs)) capture relationship-level predictors. Xrow / Xcol: Sender receiver covariates (GDP population) capturing country-level characteristics. use variables senders receivers , different covariates can specified role.

title: lame Overview
text: fit binary probit AME model sender random effects (rvar), receiver random effects (cvar), dyadic correlation (dcor), 2-dimensional latent space (R = 2). random effects capture fact countries simply active sanctioners (sender effects) frequent targets (receiver effects), latent space picks residual patterns, groups countries sanction similar targets targeted senders.
code: # Fit the AME model for binary data # Note: burn and nscan are kept small for fast vignette building. # For real analyses, use burn >= 1000 and nscan >= 5000. fit <- lame(   Y = Y,   Xdyad = Xdyad,           # dyadic covariates   Xrow = Xrow,             # sender covariates   Xcol = Xcol,             # receiver covariates   family = "binary",       # Binary probit model   rvar = TRUE,             # sender random effects   cvar = TRUE,             # receiver random effects   dcor = TRUE,             # Dyadic correlation   R = 2,                   # Multiplicative effects dimension   symmetric = FALSE,       # Directed network   burn = 100,              # Burn-in iterations   nscan = 500,             # Post-burn-in iterations   odens = 25,              # Output density (thinning)   print = FALSE,           # Suppress iteration output   plot = FALSE             # Suppress real-time plots )

title: lame Overview
text: regression coefficients tell us structural drivers sanctions. Shared IGO membership positively associated sanctions, countries embedded international institutions likely sanction , probably institutions provide information institutional mechanisms coordinate economic pressure. Distance negative coefficient, meaning geographically closer countries likely sanction one another. variance components bottom summary capture network-level heterogeneity. Sender variance (va) receiver variance (vb) indicate much countries differ baseline propensity impose receive sanctions. dyadic correlation (rho) captures reciprocity, tendency sanctions mutual.

title: lame Overview
text: lame uses MCMC, need verify sampler converged trusting results. trace_plot function shows sampled values iterations (left panels) corresponding posterior densities (right panels) parameter.  look : trace plots look like “fuzzy caterpillars,” bouncing around stable mean without long-term trends getting stuck one region. density plots smooth unimodal. 20 post-burn-samples (due small nscan used ), chains fully converged. real analysis, run much longer chains check increasing run length doesn’t substantially change posterior summaries.
code: trace_plot(fit, params = "beta")

title: lame Overview
text: gof_plot function compares observed network statistics posterior predictive distributions. tells us whether model can reproduce key structural features data, just dyad-level relationships, emergent properties like much actors vary degree (heterogeneity), whether friends--friends tend friends (transitivity), whether ties tend reciprocated (dyadic dependence).  longitudinal model, gof_plot shows statistics across time. observed values (points) overlaid posterior predictive intervals (shaded bands). observed values fall inside bands, model capturing aspect network well. fall outside, signals model missing something. instance, model consistently -predicts transitivity, might suggest higher-dimensional latent space needed.
code: gof_plot(fit)

title: lame Overview
text: multiplicative effects capture patterns association go beyond covariates additive effects explain. uv_plot function visualizes latent positions. Countries positioned near sender space (triangles) tend sanction similar targets; countries near receiver space (circles) tend sanctioned senders. Countries placed opposite sides plot tend dissimilar sanctioning patterns.  circular layout places sender positions (triangles) outer ring receiver positions (circles) inner ring, scaled magnitude. Countries larger effects placed center, indicating play distinctive role network. Countries clustered together (example, Western allies like USA Canada) share similar sanctioning profiles.
code: # Network plot showing multiplicative effects uv_plot(fit) +   ggtitle("Sanctions Network - Multiplicative Effects") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(     legend.position='none',     axis.text=element_blank(),     axis.title=element_blank()   )

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: Longitudinal Additive Multiplicative Effects Models Networks lame package builds extends Peter Hoff’s amen package, implements Additive Multiplicative Effects (AME) models cross-sectional network analysis. amen provides foundational Bayesian framework modeling network data latent factor nodal random effects, lame extends several directions: (1) longitudinal network analysis via lame() dynamic additive multiplicative effects modeled AR(1) processes, (2) support bipartite (rectangular) network structures, (3) C++ acceleration core MCMC sampling routines via Rcpp RcppArmadillo, (4) full suite S3 methods (coef(), vcov(), confint(), predict(), fitted(), residuals(), simulate()) standard R model interoperability. package includes two main functions: ame() cross-sectional network analysis lame() longitudinal network analysis dynamic effects capture temporal heterogeneity autoregressive processes. functions support unipartite (square) bipartite (rectangular) network structures.

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks

code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("netify-dev/lame", dependencies = TRUE)

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
code: library(lame)  # Load example data (creates Y, Xdyad, Xrow, Xcol in your workspace) data("vignette_data")  # Y      — list of 4 binary network matrices (35 × 35, years 1993–2000) # Xdyad  — list of dyadic covariate arrays (distance, shared IGOs) # Xrow   — list of sender covariate matrices (GDP, population) # Xcol   — list of receiver covariate matrices (GDP, population)  # Fit a basic longitudinal AME model fit <- lame(   Y = Y,                    # List of T network matrices   Xdyad = Xdyad,           # Dyadic covariates   Xrow = Xrow,             # Sender covariates   Xcol = Xcol,             # Receiver covariates   family = "binary",       # Network type   R = 2,                   # Latent dimensions   burn = 100,              # Burn-in iterations   nscan = 500              # Post-burn samples )  # Summary and diagnostics summary(fit)

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks

code: # Fit model with dynamic effects (builds on fit and data from Quick Start above) fit_dynamic <- lame(   Y = Y,   Xdyad = Xdyad,   Xrow = Xrow,   Xcol = Xcol,   family = "binary",   dynamic_ab = TRUE,       # Time-varying sender/receiver effects   dynamic_uv = TRUE,       # Time-varying latent positions   R = 2,   burn = 100,   nscan = 500,   prior = list(     rho_uv_mean = 0.9,    # High persistence for latent factors     rho_ab_mean = 0.8     # Moderate persistence for additive effects   ) )

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: Y unipartite: n x n square relational matrix. bipartite: nA x nB rectangular relational matrix nA number row nodes nB number column nodes. See family various data types. Xdyad unipartite: n x n x pd array covariates. bipartite: nA x nB x pd array covariates. Xrow unipartite: n x pr matrix nodal row covariates. bipartite: nA x pr matrix row node covariates. Xcol unipartite: n x pc matrix nodal column covariates. bipartite: nB x pc matrix column node covariates. rvar logical: fit row random effects (asymmetric case)? cvar logical: fit column random effects (asymmetric case)? dcor logical: fit dyadic correlation (asymmetric case)? Note: used bipartite networks. nvar logical: fit nodal random effects (symmetric case)? R integer: dimension multiplicative effects (can zero). bipartite networks, used default R_row R_col specified. R_row integer: bipartite networks, dimension row node multiplicative effects (defaults R) R_col integer: bipartite networks, dimension column node multiplicative effects (defaults R) mode character: either "unipartite" (default) square networks "bipartite" rectangular networks family character: one "normal","tobit","binary","ordinal","cbin","frn","rrl","poisson" - see details intercept logical: fit model intercept? symmetric logical: sociomatrix symmetric design? odmax scalar integer vector length n giving maximum number nominations node may make - used "frn" "cbin" families prior list containing hyperparameters prior distributions. Available options defaults: Sab0 Prior covariance matrix additive effects (default: diag(2)). 2x2 matrix Sab0\[1,1\] prior variance row effects, Sab0\[2,2\] prior variance column effects, -diagonals control correlation row column effects. eta0 Prior degrees freedom covariance multiplicative effects (default: 4 + 3 \* n/100, n number actors). Higher values impose stronger shrinkage latent factors. Common values: 4-10 weak shrinkage, 10-20 moderate, >20 strong shrinkage. etaab Prior degrees freedom covariance additive effects (default: 4 + 3 \* n/100). Controls shrinkage row/column random effects. Larger values shrink effects toward zero. s20 Prior variance regression coefficients (default: 1). Larger values allow larger coefficient values. s2u0 Prior variance multiplicative effects (default: 1). Suv0 Prior covariance multiplicative effects (default: identity matrix). Common usage: prior = list(Sab0 = diag(c(2, 2)), eta0 = 10) moderate shrinkage, prior = list(Sab0 = diag(c(0.5, 0.5))) tighter control. g optional scalar vector length dim(X)\[3\] g-prior regression coefficients. specified, defaults : normal family, g = nvar(Y); tobit, g = nvar(Y)*4; families, g = n, n number non-missing dyads. g-prior controls variance regression coefficients. seed random seed nscan number iterations Markov chain (beyond burn-) burn burn Markov chain odens output density Markov chain print logical: print results running? gof logical: calculate goodness fit statistics? Setting TRUE adds approximately 2-5% runtime. faster sampling without GOF overhead, set gof=FALSE use gof() model fitting. custom_gof optional function list named functions computing custom goodness--fit statistics. function must accept single matrix Y input return numeric vector. single function provided, return named vector. list functions provided, function return single value named according list names. Custom statistics computed addition default statistics. Example: custom_gof = function(Y) c(density = mean(Y > 0, na.rm = TRUE)) start_vals List previous model run containing parameter starting values new MCMC periodic_save logical: indicating whether periodically save MCMC results out_file character vector indicating name path file stored periodic_save selected. example, Apple OS out_file="~/Desktop/ameFit.rda". save_interval quantile interval indicating save post burn-phase. model.name optional string model selection output posterior_opts optional list posterior sampling options n_chains integer: number MCMC chains run (default: 1) cores integer: number cores parallel chains (default: 1) use_sparse_matrices logical: use sparse matrix storage large networks? (default: FALSE). Recommended truly sparse networks (< 10% non-zero entries).

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: Posterior Samples (full MCMC chains): BETA Regression coefficients (nscan xp matrix) VC Variance components (nscan xk matrix) GOF Goodness--fit statistics (nscan x4 matrix) Posterior Means (averaged chain): APM Additive row/sender effects (n-vector) BPM Additive column/receiver effects (m-vector); NULL symmetric networks U Multiplicative row/sender factors (n xR matrix) V Multiplicative column/receiver factors (m xR matrix); NULL symmetric networks L Eigenvalue matrix (R xR diagonal); symmetric networks YPM Posterior mean Y response scale (predictions imputing missing values) Metadata: family Model family (normal, binary, etc.) mode Network mode (unipartite bipartite) symmetric Logical indicating network symmetric R Dimension multiplicative effects Optional Posterior Samples (requested via posterior_options): U_samples Samples U (n xR xiterations array) V_samples Samples V (m xR xiterations array) a_samples Samples row effects (n xiterations matrix) b_samples Samples column effects (m xiterations matrix) Note reconstructing removed matrices: save memory, EZ (expected latent network) UVPM/ULUPM (multiplicative products) stored can reconstructed using: reconstruct_EZ(fit) - Returns linear predictor (link scale, response scale) reconstruct_UVPM(fit) - Returns U\ Generating posterior distributions: Use simulate_posterior(fit, component="UV") generate posterior samples components means stored, use posterior_options() model fitting save full posterior samples. model.name Name model (provided)

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: command provides posterior inference parameters AME models cross-sectional relational data, assuming one eight possible data types/models. function supports unipartite networks (square adjacency matrices) bipartite networks (rectangular adjacency matrices distinct row column node sets) single time point analysis. Theoretical Foundation: AME model decomposes network structure several components: $$y_{ij} = \beta'x_{ij} + a_i + b_j + u_i'v_j + \epsilon_{ij}$$ : \(\beta'x_{ij}\): Fixed effects dyadic/nodal covariates \(a_i\): Additive sender (row) effect node \(b_j\): Additive receiver (column) effect node j \(u_i'v_j\): Multiplicative interaction latent factors \(\epsilon_{ij}\): Dyadic error term (may correlated) specification generalizes social relations model (Warner et al. 1979) latent space models (Hoff et al. 2002) within unified framework. Prior Distributions: model uses conjugate semi-conjugate priors possible: Regression coefficients: \(\beta \sim N(0, g\sigma^2(X'X)^{-1})\) (g-prior) Additive effects: \((a_i, b_i)' \sim N(0, \Sigma_{ab})\) jointly Covariance: \(\Sigma_{ab} \sim IW(\eta_0, \eta_0 S_{ab0})\) (inverse-Wishart) Multiplicative effects: Hierarchical shrinkage via \(\eta_0\) Dyadic correlation: \(\rho \sim Uniform(-1, 1)\) Metropolis updates inverse-Wishart prior \(\Sigma_{ab}\) allows learning correlation sender receiver effects, capturing reciprocity patterns. Multiplicative Effects (Latent Factors): R > 0, model includes R-dimensional latent factors: Asymmetric case: \(u_i, v_j \\mathbb{R}^R\) \(u_i'v_j\) interaction Symmetric case: \(u_i = v_i\) eigendecomposition \(ULU'\) Captures homophily, transitivity, community structure R chosen via model selection set 2-3 visualization Estimation Algorithm: model uses Gibbs sampler following updates: Sample latent Z given parameters (data augmentation non-normal families) Update regression coefficients \(\beta\) via g-prior conjugate update Update additive effects (,b) jointly \(\beta\) Update covariance \(\Sigma_{ab}\) inverse-Wishart Update multiplicative effects U,V via Gibbs Metropolis-Hastings Update dyadic correlation \(\rho\) via Metropolis-Hastings Update variance \(\sigma^2\) (continuous families) Standard Model Types: following data types/models available: "normal": normal AME model (identity link: \(E[Y] = \eta\)). "tobit": tobit AME model censored continuous data. Values censored zero, appropriate non-negative continuous relational data (identity link censoring). "binary": binary probit AME model (probit link: \(P(Y=1) = \Phi(\eta)\)). "ordinal": ordinal probit AME model (cumulative probit link). intercept identifiable model. "cbin": AME model censored binary data (probit link censoring). value 'odmax' specifies maximum number links row may . "frn": AME model fixed rank nomination networks. higher value rank indicates stronger relationship. value 'odmax' specifies maximum number links row may . "rrl": AME model based row ranks. appropriate relationships across rows directly comparable terms scale. intercept, row random effects row regression effects estimable model. "poisson": overdispersed Poisson AME model count data (log link: \(E[Y] = \exp(\eta)\)). linear predictor \(\eta\) represents \(\log(\lambda)\) \(\lambda\) expected count.

title: Visualize goodness-of-fit statistics for AME and LAME models — gof_plot
text: Overview: Goodness--fit (GOF) assessment crucial network models standard residual diagnostics often inadequate capturing network dependencies. function implements posterior predictive checking : Computing key network statistics observed data Generating multiple networks model's posterior predictive distribution Computing statistics simulated networks Visualizing comparison identify model inadequacies Network Statistics Evaluated: unipartite (square) networks: sd.row (-degree heterogeneity) Standard deviation row means. High values indicate substantial variation active nodes senders/initiators. model underestimates , may missing important sender effects covariates. sd.col (-degree heterogeneity) Standard deviation column means. High values indicate substantial variation node popularity receivers. Underestimation suggests missing receiver effects popularity-related covariates. dyad.dep (Reciprocity/Mutuality) Correlation Y[,j] Y[j,]. Positive values indicate reciprocity (mutual ties likely). AME model captures dyadic correlation parameter rho. Poor fit suggests need adjust dcor parameter. triad.dep (Transitivity/Clustering) Measures tendency triadic closure (friend friend friend). Calculated correlation Y[,j] sum(Y[,k]*Y[k,j])/sqrt(n-2). AME captures multiplicative effects (U,V). Poor fit suggests increasing latent dimension R. bipartite (rectangular) networks: sd.row (Type activity variation) Standard deviation row means Type nodes. Indicates heterogeneity actively Type nodes connect Type B nodes. sd.col (Type B popularity variation) Standard deviation column means Type B nodes. Indicates heterogeneity popular Type B nodes Type nodes. four.cycles (Bipartite clustering) Count 4-cycles (rectangular paths A1-B1-A2-B2-A1). High values indicate pairs Type nodes tend connect Type B nodes. Captured bipartite multiplicative effects appropriate R_row, R_col. Interpretation Guide: Histogram plots (static models): Red vertical line: observed statistic value Blue histogram: distribution posterior predictive simulations Good fit: red line falls within bulk histogram Poor fit: red line tail outside distribution Common model inadequacies solutions: Observed sd.row/sd.col high Model underestimates degree heterogeneity. Solutions: Add row/column covariates (Xrow, Xcol), enable random effects (rvar=TRUE, cvar=TRUE), increase variance. Observed dyad.dep outside distribution Reciprocity captured well. Solutions: positive reciprocity, ensure dcor=TRUE. negative, consider transformation different family. Observed triad.dep high Clustering/transitivity underestimated. Solutions: Increase latent dimension R, add network covariates capture homophily, consider including community structure covariates. Multiple statistics showing poor fit Fundamental model misspecification. Solutions: Change family, add missing covariates, consider different model class. Mathematical Details: posterior predictive p-value statistic s : $$p = P(s(Y_{rep}) \geq s(Y_{obs}) | Y_{obs})$$ Y_rep drawn posterior predictive distribution. Values near 0 1 indicate poor fit. function visualizes full distribution rather just p-values richer diagnostics. Customization: function accepts custom GOF statistics model's custom_gof argument. automatically included plot. Custom statistics capture network features important specific application. Computational Notes: GOF computation involves simulating multiple networks, can computationally intensive. model uses networks simulated MCMC gof=TRUE specified. post-hoc GOF, use gof() function generates new simulations. Good model fit indicated : Observed statistics fall within central 95% posterior predictive distributions systematic patterns /underestimation across statistics Custom statistics (provided) also show adequate fit longitudinal models, observed trajectories track within credible bands

title: Visualize goodness-of-fit statistics for AME and LAME models — gof_plot
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas, Tosin Salau

title: Goodness of fit statistics — gof_stats
text: named numeric vector containing goodness--fit statistics. unipartite networks: sd.rowmean Standard deviation row means. Measures heterogeneity -degree centrality (sender effects). sd.colmean Standard deviation column means. Measures heterogeneity -degree centrality (receiver effects). dyad.dep Dyadic dependence/reciprocity correlation. cycle.dep Cyclic/transitive triadic dependence. trans.dep Transitive triadic dependence. bipartite networks: sd.rowmean Standard deviation row means (sender heterogeneity). sd.colmean Standard deviation column means (receiver heterogeneity). four.cycles Count four-cycles bipartite network. custom_gof provided, additional statistics included user-specified names.

title: Goodness of fit statistics — gof_stats

Missing values Y handled pairwise deletion correlations excluded matrix products triadic calculations.

text: named numeric vector containing bipartite-specific goodness--fit statistics: sd.rowmean Standard deviation row means. Measures heterogeneity -degree set nodes (sender effects). Higher values indicate variation active nodes . sd.colmean Standard deviation column means. Measures heterogeneity -degree set B nodes (receiver effects). Higher values indicate variation popular B nodes . four.cycles Count four-cycles (also called 4-paths squares) bipartite network. four-cycle occurs two nodes set (e.g., k) connect two nodes set B (e.g., j l), forming closed path: ->j->k->l->. measures tendency pairs -nodes share multiple common B-node connections, capturing form clustering specific bipartite networks. High four-cycle counts indicate connections random show patterns shared preferences co-occurrence. example, user-item network, many four-cycles suggest users like one item tend also like items co-occur .

what: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks — lame-package
title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks — lame-package
text: extension 'amen' package specifically designed longitudinal network analysis. package provides tools fitting Additive Multiplicative Effects (AME) models longitudinal/replicated relational data several key enhancements: (1) robust handling changing actor compositions across time periods, allowing networks different sets actors time point; (2) significant performance improvements C++ implementations via Rcpp RcppArmadillo; (3) specialized functions temporal network dynamics. package supports various data types including binary/network data (bin), normal relational data (nrm), ordinal relational data (ord), censored binary data (cbin), fixed-rank nomination schemes (frn), row-ranked data (rrl). Based AME framework originally developed Hoff (2009) Hoff, Fosdick, Volfovsky Stovel (2013).

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks — lame-package
text: Shahryar Minhas, Tosin Salau, Cassy Dorff Maintainer: Shahryar Minhas sminhas@example.com

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks — lame-package
code: # \donttest{ data(YX_nrm) fit <- ame(YX_nrm$Y, Xdyad = YX_nrm$X, burn = 10, nscan = 100, odens = 1,            family = "normal", print = FALSE) summary(fit) #>  #> === AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ dyad(intercept, rgpa, rsmoke, cgpa, csmoke, igrade, igpa, ismoke) + a[i] + b[j] + rho*e[ji], family = 'normal'" #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>                Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept_dyad   -2.709    0.115 -23.527       0   -2.934   -2.483 *** #> rgpa_dyad         0.219    0.101   2.165    0.03    0.021    0.418   * #> rsmoke_dyad       0.263     0.12   2.186   0.029    0.027    0.499   * #> cgpa_dyad         0.164    0.039   4.165       0    0.087    0.242 *** #> csmoke_dyad       0.112    0.056   1.995   0.046    0.002    0.222   * #> igrade_dyad       1.129    0.037  30.452       0    1.056    1.202 *** #> igpa_dyad         0.055    0.016   3.413   0.001    0.023    0.086 *** #> ismoke_dyad       0.029    0.025   1.166   0.243    -0.02    0.079     #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.853    0.120 #> cab    0.094    0.034 #> vb     0.120    0.019 #> rho    0.920    0.002 #> ve     1.053    0.020 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) # }

title: AME model fitting routine for longitudinal relational data — lame
text: Y T length list n x n relational matrices, T corresponds number replicates (time, example). See family various data types. Xdyad T length list n x n x pd arrays covariates Xrow T length list n x pr matrices nodal row covariates Xcol T length list n x pc matrices nodal column covariates rvar logical: fit row random effects (asymmetric case)? cvar logical: fit column random effects (asymmetric case)? dcor logical: fit dyadic correlation (asymmetric case)? nvar logical: fit nodal random effects (symmetric case)? R integer: dimension multiplicative effects (can zero) R_row integer: bipartite networks, dimension row node multiplicative effects (defaults R) R_col integer: bipartite networks, dimension column node multiplicative effects (defaults R) mode character: either "unipartite" (default) square networks "bipartite" rectangular networks dynamic_uv logical: fit dynamic multiplicative effects (latent factors) evolve time using AR(1) processes. TRUE, latent positions U V become time-varying, following \(U_{,t} = \rho_{uv} U_{,t-1} + \epsilon_{,t}\), epsilon follows N(0, sigma_uv^2). allows actors' positions latent social space drift smoothly time, capturing evolving network structure community dynamics. Inspired dynamic latent space models (Sewell Chen 2015, "Latent Space Models Dynamic Networks", JASA; Durante Dunson 2014, "Nonparametric Bayes Dynamic Modeling Relational Data", Biometrika). implementation uses efficient blocked Gibbs sampling C++ acceleration scalability. Default FALSE. dynamic_ab logical: fit dynamic additive effects (sender/receiver effects) evolve time using AR(1) processes. TRUE, row effects () column effects (b) become time-varying, following \(a_{,t} = \rho_{ab} a_{,t-1} + \epsilon_{,t}\). captures temporal heterogeneity actors' baseline propensities send receive ties, allowing smooth changes activity levels popularity time. example, actor's tendency form outgoing ties might gradually increase decrease across observation periods. AR(1) specification ensures temporal smoothness allowing actor-specific evolution patterns. Implementation uses conjugate updates possible C++ computational efficiency. Default FALSE. dynamic_G logical: bipartite networks, fit dynamic interaction matrix G evolves time. TRUE, rectangular interaction matrix G becomes time-varying, allowing mapping row column latent spaces change time. Default FALSE. family character: one "normal","tobit","binary","ordinal","cbin","frn","rrl","poisson" - see details intercept logical: fit model intercept? symmetric logical: sociomatrix symmetric design? odmax scalar integer vector length n giving maximum number nominations node may make - used "frn" "cbin" families prior list containing hyperparameters prior distributions. Available options defaults: Sab0 Prior covariance matrix additive effects (default: diag(2)). 2x2 matrix Sab0\[1,1\] prior variance row effects, Sab0\[2,2\] prior variance column effects, -diagonals control correlation row column effects. eta0 Prior degrees freedom covariance multiplicative effects (default: 4 + 3 \* n/100, n number actors). Higher values impose stronger shrinkage latent factors. etaab Prior degrees freedom covariance additive effects (default: 4 + 3 \* n/100). Controls shrinkage row/column random effects. rho_uv_mean dynamic_uv=TRUE: Prior mean UV AR(1) parameter (default: 0.9). Values close 1 indicate high temporal persistence. rho_uv_sd dynamic_uv=TRUE: Prior SD UV AR(1) parameter (default: 0.1). Controls uncertainty temporal dependence. sigma_uv_shape dynamic_uv=TRUE: Shape parameter inverse-gamma prior UV innovation variance (default: 2). sigma_uv_scale dynamic_uv=TRUE: Scale parameter inverse-gamma prior UV innovation variance (default: 1). rho_ab_mean dynamic_ab=TRUE: Prior mean additive effects AR(1) parameter (default: 0.8). Controls temporal smoothness sender/receiver effects. rho_ab_sd dynamic_ab=TRUE: Prior SD additive effects AR(1) parameter (default: 0.15). sigma_ab_shape dynamic_ab=TRUE: Shape parameter inverse-gamma prior additive effects innovation variance (default: 2). sigma_ab_scale dynamic_ab=TRUE: Scale parameter inverse-gamma prior additive effects innovation variance (default: 1). Common usage: prior = list(Sab0 = diag(c(1, 1)), eta0 = 10) stronger shrinkage, prior = list(rho_uv_mean = 0.95) higher temporal persistence. g optional scalar vector g-prior regression coefficients. Default p^2 p number regression parameters. g-prior controls variance regression coefficients: larger values allow larger coefficient values. Can vector length p parameter-specific control. seed random seed nscan number iterations Markov chain (beyond burn-) burn burn Markov chain odens output density Markov chain plot logical: plot results running? print logical: print results running? gof logical: calculate goodness fit statistics? start_vals List previous model run containing parameter starting values new MCMC periodic_save logical: indicating whether periodically save MCMC results out_file character vector indicating name path file stored periodic_save selected. example, Apple OS out_file="~/Desktop/ameFit.rda". save_interval quantile interval indicating save post burn-phase. model.name optional string model selection output

title: AME model fitting routine for longitudinal relational data — lame
text: command provides posterior inference parameters AME models longitudinal relational data, assuming one eight possible data types/models. model supports unipartite networks (square adjacency matrices) bipartite networks (rectangular adjacency matrices distinct row column node sets) across multiple time points. Dynamic Effects Implementation: dynamic_uv dynamic_ab parameters enable time-varying latent representations autoregressive processes. extensions particularly useful understanding network structure evolves time. Dynamic Multiplicative Effects (dynamic_uv=TRUE): latent factors U V evolve according AR(1) processes: $$U_{,k,t} = \rho_{uv} U_{,k,t-1} + \epsilon_{,k,t}$$ \(\epsilon_{,k,t} \sim N(0, \sigma_{uv}^2)\), indexes actors, k indexes latent dimensions, t indexes time. parameter \(\rho_{uv}\) controls temporal persistence (values near 1 indicate slow evolution). captures time-varying homophily, latent community structure, transitivity dynamics. Key references: Sewell & Chen (2015): Introduced dynamic latent space models actor-specific evolution rates Durante & Dunson (2014): Nonparametric Bayesian approach allowing flexible evolution network structure Hoff (2011): Hierarchical multilinear models providing theoretical foundation temporal dependencies Dynamic Additive Effects (dynamic_ab=TRUE): sender () receiver (b) effects evolve : $$a_{,t} = \rho_{ab} a_{,t-1} + \epsilon_{,t}$$ $$b_{,t} = \rho_{ab} b_{,t-1} + \eta_{,t}$$ \(\epsilon_{,t}, \eta_{,t} \sim N(0, \sigma_{ab}^2)\). models time-varying individual activity levels (outdegree) popularity (indegree). Applications include: Tracking changes node centrality time Identifying emerging influential actors Detecting declining activity patterns Modeling life-cycle effects social networks Prior Specification Dynamic Parameters: \(\rho_{uv}, \rho_{ab} \sim TruncNormal(mean, sd, 0, 1)\): Ensures stationarity AR(1) process \(\sigma_{uv}^2, \sigma_{ab}^2 \sim InverseGamma(shape, scale)\): Controls innovation variance Default priors (\(\rho_{uv}\) mean=0.9, \(\rho_{ab}\) mean=0.8) favor smooth evolution Adjust rho_*_mean closer 1 slower evolution, closer 0 rapid changes Computational Considerations: Dynamic effects increase computation ~30-50\ Memory usage scales O(nRT) dynamic_uv, O(n*T) dynamic_ab C++ implementation provides ~70\ Convergence diagnostics: Monitor rho sigma parameters carefully Effective sample sizes typically lower due temporal correlation Recommend burn >= 1000 nscan >= 20000 dynamic models Model Selection Guidelines: Use dynamic_uv dynamic_ab : Networks show clear temporal trends density clustering Individual node behavior changes systematically time Community structure evolves (merging, splitting, drift) Use dynamic_uv : Latent structure/communities change individual effects stable Focus evolving homophily clustering patterns Network shows structural reconfiguration time Use dynamic_ab : Individual heterogeneity varies overall structure stable Actors' activity/popularity changes observation period Focus individual-level temporal dynamics Bipartite Network Models: mode="bipartite", model handles rectangular adjacency matrices Y dimensions n_A x n_B, n_A n_B represent number row column nodes respectively. Static Bipartite Case: model uses separate latent factor matrices: U: n_A x R_row matrix row node latent positions V: n_B x R_col matrix column node latent positions G: R_row x R_col interaction matrix mapping latent spaces Multiplicative term: U G V' captures bipartite community structure Dynamic Bipartite Case: dynamic_uv=TRUE bipartite networks: $$U_{,k,t} = \rho_{uv} U_{,k,t-1} + \epsilon_{,k,t}$$ $$V_{j,k,t} = \rho_{uv} V_{j,k,t-1} + \eta_{j,k,t}$$ indexes row nodes, j indexes column nodes, k indexes latent dimensions. dynamic_G=TRUE, interaction matrix also evolves: $$G_{k,l,t} = \rho_G G_{k,l,t-1} + \xi_{k,l,t}$$ allowing mapping row column latent spaces change time. Key Differences Unipartite Models: dyadic correlation (rho): Bipartite edges inherently directed Separate dimensions: R_row R_col can differ row/column spaces Rectangular structure: Network density patterns differ square matrices Community interpretation: Captures affiliation patterns node types Standard AME Model Types: following describes eight standard data types/models available: "normal": normal AME model. "tobit": tobit AME model censored continuous data. Values censored zero, appropriate non-negative continuous relational data. "binary": binary probit AME model. "ordinal": ordinal probit AME model. intercept identifiable model. "cbin": AME model censored binary data.  value 'odmax' specifies maximum number links row may . "frn": AME model fixed rank nomination networks. higher value rank indicates stronger relationship. value 'odmax' specifies maximum number links row may . "rrl": AME model based row ranks. appropriate relationships across rows directly comparable terms scale. intercept, row random effects row regression effects estimable model. "poisson": overdispersed Poisson AME model count data. latent variable represents log mean Poisson distribution.

title: Simulate networks from a fitted AME model — simulate.ame
text: Mathematical Framework: AME model represents networks latent variable framework: $$Y_{ij} \sim F(Z_{ij})$$ F observation model (e.g., probit binary) Z latent network: $$Z_{ij} = \beta^T x_{ij} + a_i + b_j + u_i^T v_j + \epsilon_{ij}$$ Components: \(\beta\): regression coefficients dyadic/nodal covariates \(a_i, b_j\): additive sender receiver random effects \(u_i, v_j\): multiplicative latent factors (dimension R) \(\epsilon_{ij}\): dyadic random effects correlation \(\rho\) Uncertainty Quantification Process: simulated network k = 1, ..., nsim: Parameter Sampling: Draw parameter set \(\theta^{(k)}\) MCMC chains: Sample iteration s uniformly stored MCMC samples Extract \(\beta^{(s)}\), variance components \((v_a^{(s)}, v_b^{(s)}, v_e^{(s)}, \rho^{(s)})\) Random Effects Generation: Sample new random effects posterior distributions: \(a_i^{(k)} \sim N(0, v_a^{(s)})\) = 1, ..., n (row effects) \(b_j^{(k)} \sim N(0, v_b^{(s)})\) j = 1, ..., m (column effects) Note: sample fresh posterior variance rather using point estimates properly propagate uncertainty Latent Network Construction: Build expected latent positions: $$E[Z_{ij}^{(k)}] = \beta^{(s)T} x_{ij} + a_i^{(k)} + b_j^{(k)} + \hat{u}_i^T \hat{v}_j$$ \(\hat{u}_i, \hat{v}_j\) posterior mean latent factors Dyadic Correlation: Add correlated noise structure: $$Z_{ij}^{(k)} = E[Z_{ij}^{(k)}] + \epsilon_{ij}^{(k)}$$ \(\epsilon\) covariance structure: $$Cov(\epsilon_{ij}, \epsilon_{ji}) = \rho^{(s)} v_e^{(s)}$$ $$Var(\epsilon_{ij}) = v_e^{(s)}$$ Observation Model: Generate observed network based family: Binary: \(Y_{ij}^{(k)} = (Z_{ij}^{(k)} > 0)\) Normal: \(Y_{ij}^{(k)} = Z_{ij}^{(k)}\) Poisson: \(Y_{ij}^{(k)} \sim Poisson(\exp(Z_{ij}^{(k)}))\) families use appropriate link functions Sources Uncertainty: simulation captures three types uncertainty: Parameter uncertainty: Different MCMC samples yield different \(\beta, v_a, v_b, v_e, \rho\) Random effect uncertainty: Fresh draws \(N(0, v_a), N(0, v_b)\) simulation Dyadic uncertainty: Correlated random noise \(\epsilon_{ij}\) approach provides proper posterior predictive distributions account sources uncertainty model. variation across simulated networks reflects posterior uncertainty data generating process. Limitations: Currently, multiplicative effects (U, V) use posterior means rather sampling full posterior. complete uncertainty quantification, one need store sample full MCMC chains latent factors, require substantial additional memory. Symmetric Networks: symmetric networks, model enforces \(a_i = b_i\) \(u_i = v_i\), latent matrix Z symmetrized generating observations.

title: Simulate longitudinal networks from a fitted LAME model — simulate.lame
text: Mathematical Framework Longitudinal Networks: LAME model extends AME multiple time periods T potential temporal dependencies. time t = 1, ..., T: $$Y_{ij,t} \sim F(Z_{ij,t})$$ latent network evolves : $$Z_{ij,t} = \beta^T x_{ij,t} + a_{,t} + b_{j,t} + u_{,t}^T v_{j,t} + \epsilon_{ij,t}$$ Temporal Dynamics: LAME can incorporate three types temporal dependencies: Static Effects: Parameters constant time \(a_{,t} = a_i\), \(b_{j,t} = b_j\) t \(u_{,t} = u_i\), \(v_{j,t} = v_j\) t Dynamic Additive Effects: AR(1) process random effects $$a_{,t} = \rho_{ab} a_{,t-1} + \eta_{,t}, \quad \eta_{,t} \sim N(0, \sigma_a^2(1-\rho_{ab}^2))$$ $$b_{j,t} = \rho_{ab} b_{j,t-1} + \xi_{j,t}, \quad \xi_{j,t} \sim N(0, \sigma_b^2(1-\rho_{ab}^2))$$ \(\rho_{ab}\) temporal correlation parameter Dynamic Multiplicative Effects: AR(1) latent factors $$u_{,t} = \rho_{uv} u_{,t-1} + \omega_{,t}$$ $$v_{j,t} = \rho_{uv} v_{j,t-1} + \psi_{j,t}$$ Uncertainty Quantification Process Trajectories: simulated trajectory k = 1, ..., nsim: Step 1: Parameter Sampling Draw MCMC iteration s uniformly stored posterior samples Extract static parameters: \(\beta^{(s)}\), variance components Extract temporal parameters applicable: \(\rho_{ab}^{(s)}\), \(\rho_{uv}^{(s)}\) Step 2: Initialize t = 1 Depending start_from parameter: "posterior": Use posterior means starting values "random": Draw stationary distribution additive effects: \(a_{,1}^{(k)} \sim N(0, \sigma_a^2)\) multiplicative effects: Initialize prior Step 3: Evolve Time t = 2, ..., T: ) Update Dynamic Effects (applicable): $$a_{,t}^{(k)} = \rho_{ab}^{(s)} a_{,t-1}^{(k)} + \eta_{,t}^{(k)}$$ \(\eta_{,t}^{(k)} \sim N(0, \sigma_a^2(1-[\rho_{ab}^{(s)}]^2))\) innovation variance \(\sigma_a^2(1-\rho_{ab}^2)\) ensures stationarity b) Construct Latent Network: $$E[Z_{ij,t}^{(k)}] = \beta^{(s)T} x_{ij,t} + a_{,t}^{(k)} + b_{j,t}^{(k)} + u_{,t}^T v_{j,t}$$ c) Add Dyadic Noise: $$Z_{ij,t}^{(k)} = E[Z_{ij,t}^{(k)}] + \epsilon_{ij,t}^{(k)}$$ correlation structure preserved AME model d) Generate Observations: Apply appropriate link function based family Sources Uncertainty Longitudinal Context: Cross-sectional uncertainty (AME): Parameter uncertainty MCMC Random effect variability Dyadic noise Temporal uncertainty: Uncertainty temporal correlation parameters \(\rho_{ab}, \rho_{uv}\) Innovation noise AR(1) processes Propagation uncertainty time (compounds periods) Initial condition uncertainty: Different starting values lead different trajectories Captured start_from options Interpretation Multiple Trajectories: simulated trajectory represents one possible evolution network consistent posterior distribution. Variation across trajectories captures: Model parameter uncertainty Stochastic variation temporal evolution Accumulated uncertainty time periods ensemble trajectories provides prediction intervals widen time, reflecting increasing uncertainty longer-term forecasts. Special Considerations: Temporal Correlation: Higher \(\rho\) values create smoother trajectories persistence Stationarity: AR(1) innovation variance scaled maintain stationary marginal distributions Missing Time Points: simulating beyond observed data (n_time > T_observed), covariates recycled set zero appropriate warnings Limitations: simulate.ame, multiplicative effects currently use posterior means. Full uncertainty require storing complete MCMC chains \(u_{,t}, v_{j,t}\) time points, memory-intensive large networks long time series.

code: # \donttest{ # Create simple longitudinal network data set.seed(1) n <- 10 nms <- paste0("n", 1:n) Y_list <- list(   matrix(rnorm(n * n), n, n, dimnames = list(nms, nms)),   matrix(rnorm(n * n), n, n, dimnames = list(nms, nms)) ) diag(Y_list[[1]]) <- diag(Y_list[[2]]) <- NA fit <- lame(Y_list, family = "normal",             nscan = 50, burn = 10, odens = 1, print = FALSE, plot = FALSE)  # Simulate 10 network trajectories from posterior sims <- simulate(fit, nsim = 10) # }

title: lame 0.3.0
text: Cross-sectional network analysis via ame() support 8 distributional families: normal, binary, ordinal, tobit, Poisson, censored binary, fixed rank nomination, row-ranked likelihood. Longitudinal network analysis via lame() dynamic additive multiplicative effects modeled AR(1) processes. Support unipartite (square) bipartite (rectangular) network structures.
